content.full_text:
Re:
Farn Por-rrrcAI- PRACTTcEs C ovrnarssroN
P.O. Box 807 o 428 J Street r Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
January 28,2002
Quinn M. Barrow, City Attorney
City of Seal Beach
c/o Richards, Watson & Gershon
333 South Hope Street, 38th Floor
Los Angeles, CA 90071-1469
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4-01-260
Dear Mr. Barrow
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember
Shawn Foya regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").' Please bear in mind that this letter is based upon the facts you have presented to
us. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby
(te7s) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTIONS
1. 
Does Councilmember Boyd have a disqualifying conflict of interest that
precludes him from appointing a member to the citizens' committee formed by the Seal
Beach City Council to consult with Boeing regarding alternative land uses for the Boeing
Project Site?
2. 
Does Councilmember Boyd have a disqualifying conflict of interest that
precludes him from participating in Seal Beach City Council decisions on any of the
following matters related to Boeing's development of the Boeing Project Site:
certification of an environmental impact report; general plan amendment; development
agreement approval; zoning amendment; subdivision approval; or site plan review
approval?
t Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
A:H


File No. A-01-260
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. 
No. Councilmember Boyd does not have a disqualifuing conflict of
interest that would preclude him from appointing a committee member to the city
council's "citizens' committee" under the following circumstances: (1) Councilmember
Boyd should have no financial interest in the appointment decision; (2) There should be
no discussion and there should be no understanding between Councilmember Boyd and
the potential appointee regarding preferred uses of the Project Site; and (3) The potential
appointee should not have taken a position or otherwise expressed opinions regarding the
preferred use of the Project. Under these circumstances, it is not reasonably foreseeable
that his appointment of a particular individual will have amateial financial effect on
Boeing.
2. 
Yes. Councilmember Boyd has a disqualiffing conflict of interest as to
each of the decisions related to Boeing's development of the Boeing Project Site,
including certification of an environmental impact report, general plan amendment,
development agreement approval, zoning amendment, subdivision approval, or site plan
review approval.
tr.ACTS
The Boeing company ("Boeing") owns approximately 104.5 acres of mostly
undeveloped land in the City of Seal Beach ("the Project Site"), the value of which has
been appraised at $39 million. The Project Site is currently designated light industrial in
the Seal Beach General Plan and is zoned M-1 (light manufacturing). The Project Site is
being developed by Boeing Realty Corporation ("Boeing Realty'), a wholly owned
subsidiary of Boeing. Boeing Realty previously considered subdividing the Project Site
andutilizing it for light industrial park development. Boeing Realty has withdrawn that
proposal, however, and now intends to explore alternative land uses for the property.
councilmember Shawn Boyd's wife is an employee of Boeing Space and
Communications Division, another wholly owned subsidiary of Boeing, which is
headquartered in Seal Beach. She has been an employee for five years. you are
informed that Councilmember Boyd and his wife have not executed an agreement
waiving each other's right to community property, and that Mrs. Boyd's annual salary
from Boeing Space and Communications Division has well exceeded $1,000 in the last
twelve months.
on october 22,2001, the Seal Beach city council formed a five-member
"citizens' committee" to consult with Boeing Realty regarding alternative land uses for
the Project Site. Councilmember Boyd abstained from this decision. As proposed, each
of the five city council members will appoint one committee member.
In order to develop the Project Site, Boeing Realty must apply to the city for land
use entitlements. Although the particular applications cannot be known at this time, they
are likely to involve: environmental review; development agreement approval; general


File No. A-01-260
Page No. 3
plan amendment; zonrng amendment; and discretionary quasi-judicial approvals. In
accordance with the Seal Beach Zoning Ordinance, the city council will be responsible
for ruling on these applications, either in the first instance or in the event of an appeal of
a decision of the Planning Commission.
The City of Seal Beach has a population of less than 25,000. Boeing constitutes
the predominant employer in the city. The Boeing Company is listed on the NASDAQ.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits a public official from making,participating in making or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which
the official has a {inancial interest. (Section 87100.) Pursuant to Regulation 18700, an
eight-step analysis is applied to determine whether a public official has a conflict of
interest in a given govemmental decision.
Step One: Is the individual a 66public official?,'
A member of the Seal Beach City Council is apublic official within the meaning
of the Act. (Section 82048.)
Step Two: Is the public oflicial making, participating in making, or influencing a
governmental decision?
A public official "makes a goveilrmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or
her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contracfual agreement on behalf of
his or her agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates
in making a govemmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive review, the official negotiates, advises or
makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision.
(Section 87100; Regulation 18702.2.) All of the activities you have asked about
constitute making and/or participating in making a govemmental decision. (Regulation
18702.1(a).) This includes appointing a member to the citizens' committee, participating
in consideration of and voting on the application of Boeing to certi$ an environmental
impact report, for a general plan amendment, development agreement approval, zoning
amendments, subdivision approval or site plan review approval.
Step Three: What is the 66economic interest,, of the public official?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:


a
a
a
a
o
File No. A-01-260
Page No. 4
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment 2 of $2,000 or more (Section S7103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, parhrer,
trustee, employee, orholds anyposition of management (Section S7103(d);
Regulation 1 8703. I (b));
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); Regulation
18703.2);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (Section 87103(c); Regulation 13703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $320 or more within 12 months prior to the decision
(Section 87 1 03(e); Regulation 187 03.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (Section 87103; Regulation I8703.5).
Under the facts provided, by reason of his community property interest in his
spouse's income, Councilmember Boyd has an economic interest in Boeing Space and
Communications Division as a source of income. (Section 87103(c); see also, Section
82029.) Because Boeing Space and Communications Division is a wholly owned
subsidiary of Boeing, Councilmember Boyd also has an economic interest in Boeing as a
source of income. (Reg. 18703.1(c).)
Step Four: Are the public official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved
in the decision?
A source of income is directly involved in a govemmental decision if it:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
'l(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
2 An indirect investrnent or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an
official or dependent child, by an agent on behalf of a public official, or by a business entity oi trust in
which the official, the official's immediate family, or their agents own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a
l0-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.)


File No. A-01-260
Page No. 5
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or conkact with, the
subject person." (Reg. 1 870a. 1(a).)
With respect to the decision to appoint a committee member to the 'ocitizens'
committee" to consult with Boeing Realty regarding alternative land uses for the Project
Site, it appears that Councilmember Boyd's economic interests are indirectly involved in
the decision.
However, with respect to the other decisions about which you have asked, i.e.
certification of an environmental impact report, general plan amendment, development
agreement approval, zoning amendment, subdivision approval, and site plan review
approval, Boeing would either initiate, be a named party to, or be the subject of any such
proceedings. Therefore, Councilmember Boyd's economic interests would be directly
involved in those decisions.
Steps Five and Six: Will the financial effect of the decision on the official's economic
interest be material and reasonably foreseeable?
Once a public official identifies his or her relevant economic interests, the official
must evaluate whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on any of those economic interests. This determination takes two steps.
First, the official must find and apply the applicable materiality standard set forth in
Commission regulations. (Regulation 18700(bX5), Regulation 18705, et seq.) After
finding the applicable materiality standard, the official must then decide whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the standard will be met. (Regulation 18700(b)(6).)
Appointing a committee Member to the city council's "citizens' committee"
Source of Income - Indirect Involvement
Regulation 18706 provides that "[a] material financial effect on an economic
interest is reasonably foreseeable, within the meaning of Govemment Code section
87103, if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards (see Cal.
Code Regs.,tit.2 $$ 18704, 18705) applicable to that economic interest will be met as a
result of the governmental decision." A frnancial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FppC ops. 198.) The
materiality standard applicable to a source of income that is indirectly involved with a
govemmental decision is set forth in Regulation 18705.3(b), which states:
"Indirectly involved sources of income
"(1) Sources of income which are business entities. If the
source of income is a business entity, apply the materiality
standards stated in Title 2, Califomia Code of Regulations,
section 18705.1(c)."
Regulation 18705.1(c) provides, in pertinent part:


File No. A-01-260
Page No. 6
"(c) Indirectly involved business entities. The following
materiality standards apply when a business entity in which
a public official has an economic interest is indirectly
involved in a governmental decision. If more than one of
the following subdivisions is applicable to the business
entity in question, apply the subdivision with the highest
dollar thresholds.
fl. . .'lT
"(3) If the business entity is listed, or meets the financial
criteria for listing, on the NASDAQ/AMEX, the financial
effect of a governmental decision on the business entity is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The govefirmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease to the business entity's gross revenues for a
fiscal year in the amount of $300,000 or more; or
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing
or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $100,000 or more; or
"(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $300,000
or more."
The critical question in this analysis is whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
Councilmember Boyd's decision to appoint an individual to the "citizens' committee" to
consult with Boeing Realty regarding alternative land uses for the Project Site will have a
material financial effect on Boeing.
Appointing an individual is considered making a govemmental decision under the
Act. Therefore, an official's appointment decision, like any other governmental decision,
should be examined independently to determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that
the appointment, itself will have a material financial effect on the official's economic
interests.
A series ofadvice letters discusses the issue offoreseeability in the context of
appointment decisions.
In the Benjamin Advice Letter, No. 4-86-148, we stated thatparticipation in the
appointment process was permissible so long as the "disqualified public official does not


a
File No. A-01-260
Page No. 7
seek in any way to influence the decision of [the appointee] as to the specific decision as
to which disqualification is required."
In the Lofgren Advice Letter, No. A-96-042,we advised that the mayor of Folsom
was not precluded from making appointnents to the City of Folsom Redevelopment
Agency Citizens Advisory Committee and the Historical Area Committee based on his
ownership of property within the redevelopment area and historic district area. Absent a
specific agreement with the appointees, it was not foreseeable that the appointments
would have a material financial effect on the mayor's economic interests. (See also,
Ungar Advice Letter,No. A-93-277; McHugh Advice Lette4No. A-93-142.)
Several letters conclude that an appointment decision will not create a conflict for an
official in circumstances where:
The official has no financial interest in the appointment decision;
There is no understanding between the official and the appointee as to how the
appointee will vote; and
The appointee has not taken a position on the issue or otherwise expressed
intentions as to how he or she might vote on particular issues.
For example, the Dorsey Advice Letter, No. ,4.-89-396, stated that acity council
member who resigned from a redevelopment agency because of conflicts of interest,
could vote on his replacement as long as the factors above were met. The Sauer Advice
Letter, No. A-95-373, advised that despite having a conflict of interest in decisions
pertaining to the proposed affiliation between two hospital districts, a member of one of
the hospital district's board of directors could participate in the appointment of an
individual to act as a representative in discussions on the affiliation proposal as long as
the above factors were in place.
Our advice in this letter and those discussed above is premised on the assumption
that an appointee will act independently, exercising his or her own judgment to decide
issues rather than merely carrying out the will of the appointing power.
In this case, you have given us no information to suggest that the "citizens'
committee" will have any actual voting power, or that it will have any authority over any
decisions that will actually impact Boeing. Rather, you imply that the purpose of the
"citizens' committee" is to act in an advisory capacity to Boeing, offering advice
regarding alternative uses for the Project Site. You have given no indication whether
Boeing has agreed to work with the committee, or to what extent the city council has
agreed to accept the recommendations of the committee.
Nevertheless, Councilmember Boyd will have no conflict of interest if he appoints
an individual to the "citizens' committee" following generally the above-stated factors,
i.e. (1) Councilmember Boyd should have no financial interest in the appointment
decision; (2) There should have been no discussion and should be no understanding
between Councilmember Boyd and the potential appointee regarding preferred uses of


File No. A-01-260
Page No. 8
the Project Site; and (3) The potential appointee should not have taken a position or
otherwise expressed opinions regarding the preferred use of the Project Site. Under these
circumstances, it is not reasonably foreseeable that his appointment of a particular
individual will have amateial financial effect on Boeing. (See generally, McAndrews
Advice Letteq No. 4-99-2 I 3.)
Questions Involving Land Use Issues
Source of Income from Business Entity - Directly Involved
The materiality standard used for a source of income that is a business entity is
that applicable to an economic interest in a business entity. The materiality standard
applicable to a business entity that is directly involved with a governmental decision is
set forth in Regulation 18705.1(b), which states:
"(b) Directly involved business entities
"(1) General Rule: Unless the exception in subdivision
(bX2) of this regulation applies, the financial effects of a
governmental decision on a business entity which is
directly involved in the govemmental decision is presumed
to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof
that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have any financial effect on the business
entity."
The exception in subdivision (bX2) of the regulation does not apply in this case.
You ask whether Councilmember Boyd would have a disqualiffing conflict of
interest that would preclude him from participating in city council decisions regarding
use of the Project Site in decisions such as certification of an environmental impact
report; general plan amendment; development agreement approval; zoning amendment;
subdivision approval; or site plan review approval. Because the financial effects of a
governmental decision on any of these issues is presumed to be material, Councilmember
Boyd has a disqualifying conflict of interest that would preclude him from participating
in any of these decisions, absent rebuttal of the presumption of materiality by proof that
any of the decisions will not have any financial effect on Boeing.
Steps Seven and Eight: Exceptions
We have not gone on to analyze the latter two steps. Step seven is an exception
that applies where the reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on the
official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
and step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legally required to
participate in the decision. You have not provided any facts to suggest that these
exceptions may apply. You have also provided no specific facts that would permit us to
analyze whether the predominant industry exception applies in this case.


File No. A-01-260
Page No. 9
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
B. Armstrong
Legal Division
HBAjg
I:\Adviceltrs\A-0 1 -260

seek in any way to influence the decision of [the appointee] as to the specific decision as
to which disqualification is required."
In the Lofgren Advice Letter, No. A-96-042,we advised that the mayor of Folsom
was not precluded from making appointnents to the City of Folsom Redevelopment
Agency Citizens Advisory Committee and the Historical Area Committee based on his
ownership of property within the redevelopment area and historic district area. Absent a
specific agreement with the appointees, it was not foreseeable that the appointments
would have a material financial effect on the mayor's economic interests. (See also,
Ungar Advice Letter,No. A-93-277; McHugh Advice Lette4No. A-93-142.)
Several letters conclude that an appointment decision will not create a conflict for an
official in circumstances where:
The official has no financial interest in the appointment decision;
There is no understanding between the official and the appointee as to how the
appointee will vote; and
The appointee has not taken a position on the issue or otherwise expressed
intentions as to how he or she might vote on particular issues.
For example, the Dorsey Advice Letter, No. ,4.-89-396, stated that acity council
member who resigned from a redevelopment agency because of conflicts of interest,
could vote on his replacement as long as the factors above were met. The Sauer Advice
Letter, No. A-95-373, advised that despite having a conflict of interest in decisions
pertaining to the proposed affiliation between two hospital districts, a member of one of
the hospital district's board of directors could participate in the appointment of an
individual to act as a representative in discussions on the affiliation proposal as long as
the above factors were in place.
Our advice in this letter and those discussed above is premised on the assumption
that an appointee will act independently, exercising his or her own judgment to decide
issues rather than merely carrying out the will of the appointing power.
In this case, you have given us no information to suggest that the "citizens'
committee" will have any actual voting power, or that it will have any authority over any
decisions that will actually impact Boeing. Rather, you imply that the purpose of the
"citizens' committee" is to act in an advisory capacity to Boeing, offering advice
regarding alternative uses for the Project Site. You have given no indication whether
Boeing has agreed to work with the committee, or to what extent the city council has
agreed to accept the recommendations of the committee.
Nevertheless, Councilmember Boyd will have no conflict of interest if he appoints
an individual to the "citizens' committee" following generally the above-stated factors,
i.e. (1) Councilmember Boyd should have no financial interest in the appointment
decision; (2) There should have been no discussion and should be no understanding
between Councilmember Boyd and the potential appointee regarding preferred uses of
the Project Site; and (3) The potential appointee should not have taken a position or
otherwise expressed opinions regarding the preferred use of the Project Site. Under these
circumstances, it is not reasonably foreseeable that his appointment of a particular
individual will have amateial financial effect on Boeing. (See generally, McAndrews
Advice Letteq No. 4-99-2 I 3.)
Questions Involving Land Use Issues
Source of Income from Business Entity - Directly Involved
The materiality standard used for a source of income that is a business entity is
that applicable to an economic interest in a business entity. The materiality standard
applicable to a business entity that is directly involved with a governmental decision is
set forth in Regulation 18705.1(b), which states:
"(b) Directly involved business entities
"(1) General Rule: Unless the exception in subdivision
(bX2) of this regulation applies, the financial effects of a
governmental decision on a business entity which is
directly involved in the govemmental decision is presumed
to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof
that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the governmental
decision will have any financial effect on the business
entity."
The exception in subdivision (bX2) of the regulation does not apply in this case.
You ask whether Councilmember Boyd would have a disqualiffing conflict of
interest that would preclude him from participating in city council decisions regarding
use of the Project Site in decisions such as certification of an environmental impact
report; general plan amendment; development agreement approval; zoning amendment;
subdivision approval; or site plan review approval. Because the financial effects of a
governmental decision on any of these issues is presumed to be material, Councilmember
Boyd has a disqualifying conflict of interest that would preclude him from participating
in any of these decisions, absent rebuttal of the presumption of materiality by proof that
any of the decisions will not have any financial effect on Boeing.
Steps Seven and Eight: Exceptions
We have not gone on to analyze the latter two steps. Step seven is an exception
that applies where the reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on the
official's economic interest is not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
and step eight is an exception that applies when the official is legally required to
participate in the decision. You have not provided any facts to suggest that these
exceptions may apply. You have also provided no specific facts that would permit us to
analyze whether the predominant industry exception applies in this case.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. 
Does Councilmember Boyd have a disqualifying conflict of interest that
precludes him from appointing a member to the citizens' committee formed by the Seal
Beach City Council to consult with Boeing regarding alternative land uses for the Boeing
Project Site?
2. 
Does Councilmember Boyd have a disqualifying conflict of interest that
precludes him from participating in Seal Beach City Council decisions on any of the
following matters related to Boeing's development of the Boeing Project Site:
certification of an environmental impact report; general plan amendment; development
agreement approval; zoning amendment; subdivision approval; or site plan review
approval?
A:H

CONCLUSION: 1. 
No. Councilmember Boyd does not have a disqualifuing conflict of
interest that would preclude him from appointing a committee member to the city
council's "citizens' committee" under the following circumstances: (1) Councilmember
Boyd should have no financial interest in the appointment decision; (2) There should be
no discussion and there should be no understanding between Councilmember Boyd and
the potential appointee regarding preferred uses of the Project Site; and (3) The potential
appointee should not have taken a position or otherwise expressed opinions regarding the
preferred use of the Project. Under these circumstances, it is not reasonably foreseeable
that his appointment of a particular individual will have amateial financial effect on
Boeing.
2. 
Yes. Councilmember Boyd has a disqualiffing conflict of interest as to
each of the decisions related to Boeing's development of the Boeing Project Site,
including certification of an environmental impact report, general plan amendment,
development agreement approval, zoning amendment, subdivision approval, or site plan
review approval.
tr.ACTS
The Boeing company ("Boeing") owns approximately 104.5 acres of mostly
undeveloped land in the City of Seal Beach ("the Project Site"), the value of which has
been appraised at $39 million. The Project Site is currently designated light industrial in
the Seal Beach General Plan and is zoned M-1 (light manufacturing). The Project Site is
being developed by Boeing Realty Corporation ("Boeing Realty'), a wholly owned
subsidiary of Boeing. Boeing Realty previously considered subdividing the Project Site
andutilizing it for light industrial park development. Boeing Realty has withdrawn that
proposal, however, and now intends to explore alternative land uses for the property.
councilmember Shawn Boyd's wife is an employee of Boeing Space and
Communications Division, another wholly owned subsidiary of Boeing, which is
headquartered in Seal Beach. She has been an employee for five years. you are
informed that Councilmember Boyd and his wife have not executed an agreement
waiving each other's right to community property, and that Mrs. Boyd's annual salary
from Boeing Space and Communications Division has well exceeded $1,000 in the last
twelve months.
on october 22,2001, the Seal Beach city council formed a five-member
"citizens' committee" to consult with Boeing Realty regarding alternative land uses for
the Project Site. Councilmember Boyd abstained from this decision. As proposed, each
of the five city council members will appoint one committee member.
In order to develop the Project Site, Boeing Realty must apply to the city for land
use entitlements. Although the particular applications cannot be known at this time, they
are likely to involve: environmental review; development agreement approval; general
plan amendment; zonrng amendment; and discretionary quasi-judicial approvals. In
accordance with the Seal Beach Zoning Ordinance, the city council will be responsible
for ruling on these applications, either in the first instance or in the event of an appeal of
a decision of the Planning Commission.
The City of Seal Beach has a population of less than 25,000. Boeing constitutes
the predominant employer in the city. The Boeing Company is listed on the NASDAQ.

Please bear in mind that this letter is based upon the facts you have presented to us. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (te7s) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS 1. Does Councilmember Boyd have a disqualifying conflict of interest that precludes him from participating in Seal Beach City Council decisions on any of the following matters related to Boeing's development of the Boeing Project Site: certification of an environmental impact report; general plan amendment; development agreement approval; zoning amendment; subdivision approval; or site plan review approval? t Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. A:H File No. A-01-260 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS 1. No. Councilmember Boyd does not have a disqualifuing conflict of interest that would preclude him from appointing a committee member to the city council's "citizens' committee" under the following circumstances: (1) Councilmember Boyd should have no financial interest in the appointment decision; (2) There should be no discussion and there should be no understanding between Councilmember Boyd and the potential appointee regarding preferred uses of the Project Site; and (3) The potential appointee should not have taken a position or otherwise expressed opinions regarding the preferred use of the Project. Under these circumstances, it is not reasonably foreseeable that his appointment of a particular individual will have amateial financial effect on Boeing. 2. Yes. Councilmember Boyd has a disqualiffing conflict of interest as to each of the decisions related to Boeing's development of the Boeing Project Site, including certification of an environmental impact report, general plan amendment, development agreement approval, zoning amendment, subdivision approval, or site plan review approval. tr.ACTS The Boeing company ("Boeing") owns approximately 104.5 acres of mostly undeveloped land in the City of Seal Beach ("the Project Site"), the value of which has been appraised at $39 million. The Project Site is currently designated light industrial in the Seal Beach General Plan and is zoned M-1 (light manufacturing). The Project Site is being developed by Boeing Realty Corporation ("Boeing Realty'), a wholly owned subsidiary of Boeing. Boeing Realty previously considered subdividing the Project Site andutilizing it for light industrial park development.
